reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware reasoning about (i) ARC surface-vs-core measurement limitations and (ii) temporal ordering of thermal events relative to a defined triggering criterion, yielding multiple admissible conclusions without requiring external facts. Decoys are largely plausible and fail in distinct ways (definition/timing error, measurement over-claim, mechanistic overreach), though a couple options lean on absolute modal language (“best explained,” “definitively”) that can introduce mild stylistic cueing and reduce single-fault purity. Overall it provides solid expert discrimination by rewarding careful scope control and resisting mechanistic extrapolation from limited observations.

The prompt tightly constrains the intended judgment: whether a “temperature-shifted same SEI mechanism” is sufficient, and what specific SiOx-linked anode mechanism must be added to explain both triggering temperature and the narrower onset-to-triggering gap. It elicits boundary-aware mechanistic reasoning (SEI decomposition vs decomposition–regeneration buffering) rather than generic safety commentary, and includes quantitative anchors (onset/trigger values) that improve diagnostic separation. The main weakness is that as a short-answer item it lacks decoy options, so discriminative signal depends on scorer rubric quality rather than forced-choice structure.
